data.title: Infineon: The AI Power Conversion Wave Is Coming
data.description:
Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY) (OTCQX:IFNNF) jumped nearly 7% after reporting strong Q4 2025 results, extending the stock’s gain to 10.9% since my last buy-rated report. While this trails both my prior 13% upside estimate and the S&P 500’s 13% return over the same period, Infineon continues to benefit from robust demand in power semiconductors and automotive applications. In this report, I break down the Q4 earnings, evaluate Infineon’s growth outlook amid elevated chip demand, and assess whether the stock still justifies a buy rating.

Infineon Technologies Results Do Not Impress

Fourth quarter results showed year-over-year growth of 1% and sequential growth of 6% to €3.94 billion. Segment results decreased 13.8% to €717 million as margins contracted 300 basis points.

In the Automotive segment, revenues declined by 2% to €1.92 billion as volume growth was offset by less favorable pricing. This also led to profits declining 15.7% as margins declined from 25.9% to 22.4%. Given the challenges that the automotive industry is facing, the stability in sales seems good. However, the sales included some sales being pulled into the quarter driven by the expiry of subsidies.

The Green Industrial Power segment saw an 8% decline to €463 million, driven by macroeconomic weakness that affected industrial demand and a weaker market for renewable energy, and as a result, fell 38% as margins declined from 22.1% to 14.9%.

Power & Sensor Systems sales increased 14% to €1.19 billion on tripling AI-related demand and an improved market environment for consumer electronics. Profits increased 23% to €179 million, with margins expanding from 14% to 15.1%.

Connected Secure Systems sales declined 9% to €369 million, driven by weakness in security and the Internet of Things or IoT. Profits declined 27% to €45 million as margins dropped 310 basis points to 12.2%.

Generally, the results were not impressive, as sales declines in security, IoT, renewables, and industrial could not offset improved demand patterns for data center power delivery and consumer electronics. That also removes some of the higher margin business from the mix, leading to a decline in margins.

Guidance Is Not A Bullish Guide Either

For the first quarter, Infineon is guiding for €3.6 billion in sales and mid-to-high teens margins. For the full year, modest sales growth is expected with margins in the high teens. As a reference, in FY25 the company reported €14.7 billion in sales and margins of 17.5%. Adjusted free cash flow was €1.8 billion and is expected to decline to €1.6 billion in FY26. I believe that with low growth and margins comparable to the FY25 results, as well as lower adjusted free cash flow, there are few bright spots. The only positive would be that free cash flow is expected to be €1.1 billion compared to a €1.1 billion free cash flow burn in the year prior, driven by the absence of free cash flow pressure from the acquisition of Marvell’s Automotive Ethernet business.

So, overall the guidance is not impressive, but we also note it includes a €400 million currency driven headwind.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Infineon Technologies

Currently we are seeing that macroeconomic headwinds are offsetting sales growth and eroding margins despite AI-powered delivery as a strong emerging sales driver. The other risk is the currency exchange rates.

Moving to growth drivers, the position that Infineon has for power semiconductors can be leveraged in power delivery to data centers, renewable energy, automation, connectivity, and the automotive market. Those are the long-term growth pillars for the company, as devices carry more chip content these days and power efficiency is becoming increasingly more important. For AI related power conversion where Infineon provides semiconductors from the grid to the CPU core, Infineon believes its products can drive system efficiency from 85% to 90%. This is quite important. Currently many data center operators are securing access to energy. However, for scalability, it is also important that system efficiency improves.

Infineon Technologies Fairly Valued, But Multiple Expansion Is Justified

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Since we are in FY26 and I believe looking ahead at expectations for FY27, the stock seems to be fairly valued against the company’s median EV/EBITDA multiple. However, I do believe that a higher multiple can be justified given the company’s AI growth opportunities. If we remain at some level of conservatism and value the stock at the average of the targets supported by expected results for FY26, we get to a price target of around $49, implying 16.5% upside. In a more bullish scenario, we factor in forward earnings of FY27, and that gives us a price target of around $58.50 and 39% upside.

I believe that margins are expected to improve in the years ahead with significant improvement in the cash conversion from EBITDA to FCF as CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow is set to reduce substantially. Shareholder returns definitely do not seem impressive, but we note that debt is set to come down substantially through a combination of debt repayments and refinancing.

Conclusion: Infineon Technologies Remains Attractive For Long-Term Investors

The financial results and guidance of Infineon Technologies are not particularly exciting to me. However, I do believe that despite macroeconomic headwinds in some end markets there is substantial demand for power semiconductors and moreover for energy efficient ones. That is where Infineon Technologies excels and with AI and data center driven power demand, I do believe that Infineon Technologies has a strong investment case.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing: Double-Digit Dividend Hike Proves This Is A Dividend King Worth Owning
data.description:
Introduction

With the current state of the economy due to inflation, high-interest rates, potential AI disruptions, and a weakening labor market, it's quite refreshing to own dividend stocks. Particularly, companies with long pay histories, such as Dividend Aristocrats & Dividend Kings.

Especially, those who show their financial strength by raising their dividends double-digits, like Automatic Data Processing (ADP) did recently. This not only helps blunt the blow from inflation; this ensures the likelihood of seeing future increases from a strong, cash-flowing business.

In this article, I'll discuss ADP's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm initiating a buy rating on Automatic Data Processing.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, Automatic Data Processing is a Dividend King, meaning they have a long history of paying dividends. The company was founded in 1949 and went public over six decades ago in 1961.

ADP is a global payroll company that operates in over 140 countries. They allow businesses to (operate) efficiently by managing clients, personnel, tax information, and payroll for businesses of all sizes. ADP has over 1 million clients, which include behemoths like Amazon (AMZN) and Dell Technologies (DELL).

Latest Earnings

Automatic Data Processing reported their first quarter earnings for fiscal year 2026 on October 29th and managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines.

EPS of $2.49 beat by a solid $0.04, while revenue exceeded expectations by $50 million, amounting to $5.18 billion. Both also increased from the previous quarter and year. EPS increased from $2.26 in Q4, and revenue from $5.13 billion.

Year-over-year, both rose 6.87% and 7.25%, respectively, solid considering the challenging economic backdrop. Despite their solid showing and growth, ADP's price is down nearly 17% in the past year.

Me, personally, I believe this is an overreaction from the market due to threats of AI disruption. However, ADP has been integrating AI into their business with the launch of ADP Assist, expected to drive growth by integrating AI capabilities into products like solving payroll anomalies.

The company has also been making acquisitions. The most recent acquisition was Pequity, a software management company launched in 2019. This is expected to broaden ADP's capabilities as well as help clients support compensation planning and make informed pay decisions.

In FY'25, they acquired WorkForce Software, expected to simplify client operations, increase visibility, and lower costs. So, from a fundamental standpoint, ADP seems to be firing on all cylinders.

Segment Performance

Automatic Data Processing currently operates in two segments: Employer Services & PEO Services. Both saw revenue growth of 7% despite margins declining double and triple-digits year-over-year.

Their Employer Services segment margin decline was related to their WorkForce Software acquisition, resulting in a (decline) of 50 basis points. This brought margins at the end of Q1 to 35.2%.

PEO's decline was due to increases in zero-margin benefit pass-through costs, workers' compensation & insurance, and higher marketing expenses. The segment's margins stood at 13%.

However, management still reaffirmed guidance for the full year. Revenue is expected to see 5% to 6% growth from $20.6 billion, while their bottom line is expected to see slightly higher growth from $10.01 in a range of 8% to 10%.

Double-Digit Dividend Hike

Despite potential AI disruptions, Automatic Data Processing showed its financial prowess with a strong 10.4% dividend hike earlier this week. This represented their 51st consecutive dividend increase, adding to their already impressive streak.

This also shows management's confidence in the business going forward. What's most impressive about ADP's dividend raise is this is amidst economic uncertainty and a time when other quality Dividend Kings have chosen to be more conservative.

Case in point, Dividend Kings Pepsi (PEP) & Procter & Gamble (PG) both raised their dividends a solid 5% earlier this year, lower than the prior year. And while this is likely to do with tariffs and economic uncertainty placing downward pressure on consumers, ADP's increase should give investors and shareholders confidence going forward.

Moreover, their dividends remain well-covered by their cash flows. For FY'25, ADP ended with a payout ratio of 50.4%. And strong cash flow growth and dividend raises are likely to continue, partly thanks to the company's frequent share repurchases.

Although buybacks have slowed a bit since 2022, likely due to inflation, the Dividend King has repurchased more than $1 billion in subsequent years. For 2025, they took 4.4 million shares off the books. This not only ensures the dividend will likely remain safe, but that cash flow and earnings will continue to see solid growth for the foreseeable future.

Balance Sheet

Automatic Data Processing's balance sheet was in good shape, as evidenced by their A credit rating. Additionally, the company has no debt maturing until 2028, with $4 billion worth of notes due.

Total long-term debt stood at $3.976, minimal in comparison to their market cap of over $100 billion. Liquidity was robust with nearly $2.5 billion in cash & equivalents.

Fairly Valued

According to Fast Graphs, ADP looks fairly valued here as they currently trade slightly below their normal P/E of 25.45x. The stock also trades near their 52-week low of $249.68.

So, for long-term investors, I think ADP is worth dollar-cost averaging into here and adding on any signs of further share price weakness. And the upside to their price target of $332 over the next 3 years is solid.

Risks & Bottom Line

Although the government shutdown seems to be ending soon, delayed impacts and the potential to see further economic weakening could result in more downside for Automatic Data Processing.

With the recent uptick in job losses, I suspect we could see a potential recession in the next year or two, as this is seemingly a sign of cracks starting to show. I personally believe even if we manage a soft landing, things will get worse before they get better, particularly in the labor market.

However, ADP continues to be one of the most resilient companies, and I expect despite share price pullbacks, they will continue to be a solid long-term wealth compounder.

data.title: Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
This note will highlight the results of the Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund for Q3’ 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, ongoing inflation concerns in the U.S., Mideast violence, and significant government policy changes in the U.S. and elsewhere that are altering global trade and financial flows. Most equity indices worldwide delivered positive returns in the September quarter. Returns from bonds in the U.S. were positive.

Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund paid ordinary dividends of $0.335 per I share during Q3’2025, +8.8% versus Q3’24. For the trailing 12 months, your fund paid ordinary dividends of $1.27 per I share, +5.5% vs the prior year comparable period. The dividends per share were lower for A and C shares, to account for varying class-specific expenses. Your fund also paid capital gains dividends as follows in Q4’24: short-term capital gains of 5.5¢/share and long-term capital gains of 16.6¢/ share. Including the capital gains dividends, trailing 4-quarter dividends of $1.49 per I share were +24.2% vs the dividend of $1.20 per share for the 4 quarters ending September 30, 2024.

Approximately 90% of the dividends paid by your fund in 2024 were characterized as "Qualifying Dividends" for federal income tax purposes, leading to a lower tax rate on these payments. More than 80% of your Investment Income Builder’s equity holdings increased dividends in local currencies in 2024, a slightly higher percentage compared to the prior year. A stronger $US was a headwind to your fund’s dividend growth in calendar 2024. A weaker $US has been a tailwind this year since many of your fund’s equity holdings pay dividends in foreign currencies that we subsequently convert to dollars. Other factors may impact the distributable cash total.

The net asset value of Investment Income Builder’s I shares increased by +$5.24 per share ($26.87 to $32.11) over the trailing 12 months and increased by $1.85 per share ($30.26 to $32.11) during the September quarter. Your fund’s 9/30/25 net asset value was reduced by the aforementioned 22¢/share capital gains dividends paid in Q4’24. Investment Income Builder’s I share return of 7.24%% for Q3 2025 exceeded the blended benchmark return of 5.94% for the September quarter (the blended benchmark is 75% MSCI World Index and 25% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index). For the 12-month period ending September 30, 2025, Income Builder’s I share return of 25.96% exceeded the blended benchmark return of 13.59%. Performance comparisons of Investment Income Builder to its blended benchmark over various periods are shown on page 1.

The quarter ending September 30, 2025, was the 91st full calendar quarter since the inception of Thornburg Investment Income Builder in December 2002. In 67 of these quarters the fund delivered a positive total return. The fund has delivered positive total returns in 17 of its 22 calendar years of existence.

Importantly, for an investor reinvesting dividends Thornburg Investment Income Builder has delivered an average annualized total return of 10.18% (I shares extended) since its inception 22.75 years ago, split between quarterly income distributions and share price appreciation.

On a $100,000 initial investment a hypothetical investor owning Thornburg Investment Income Builder A shares and taking all ordinary dividends in cash (no reinvestment of quarterly dividends) would have received total cash dividends of $192,485 over these 91 quarters. In addition, the current value of the original investment stood at $289,652 as of September 30, 2025.

Listed at right in descending order are the 25 largest equity holdings in the fund as of September 30, 2025, along with their share price changes in $US for the first nine months of 2025 and calendar year 2024. Also noted are dividend yields at closing September 30, 2025, stock prices and the trailing 5-year growth rate of each firm’s dividend/share in local currency. Together, these 25 firms comprised approximately 64% of the fund’s total assets as of September 30, 2025. Cash and more than 240 interest-bearing debt and hybrid securities comprised around 20% of fund assets, and 33 other common equities comprised a total of approximately 16% of fund assets. Individual position sizes of the 25 equities listed range from 6.4% of fund assets (Orange) to approximately 1.1% for those shown near the bottom of the list.

Twenty-three equity investments contributed positive returns of at least 0.10% to overall Income Builder Fund portfolio performance during Q3’2025. The most positive equity contributors to quarterly performance included telecommunications network operators Orange (OTC:ORANY), Vodafone (VOD), Singapore Telecommunications (OTCPK:SNGNF) and Zegona Communications (OTC:ZEGLF); information technology firms Broadcom (AVGO), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM); European financials BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF), NN Group (OTCPK:NNGPF), Bank of Ireland (OTCPK:BKRIF), and Generali; U.S. financials Citigroup (C) and Regions Financial (RF); North American utilities TC Energy (TRP), Enbridge (ENB) and Home Depot (HD) and miner Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF).

One of your fund’s equity investments, Equitable Holdings (EQH), made a negative contribution to portfolio performance of more than -0.10% during Q3’2025. While providing more modest performance headwinds, AT&T (T), Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGF), LyondellBasell (LYB), HCL Technologies, and DHL Group were also negative performers in the September quarter.

Investment Income Builder’s bond holdings delivered positive returns during Q3’2025. Ten-year U.S. Treasury bond yields dropped from 4.22% to 4.15% in the quarter, after rising from 3.88% to 4.57% during calendar 2024. Corporate and asset-backed bond yield spreads to U.S. Treasury bonds tightened in Q2’25. The market yield of the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index decreased from 7.06% to 6.69% during the September quarter (yield to lower of call price or maturity). On September 30, 2025, the $74 trillion Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index showed an average maturity of 8.3 years and a yield of 3.48%, the latter decreasing by -0.2% over the September quarter. Developed world bond yields outside the U.S. are mostly below those available in the U.S. market.

Readers of this commentary who are long-time shareholders of Thornburg Investment Income Builder will recall that the interest-bearing debt portion of the fund’s portfolio has varied over time, ranging from less than 9% in 2015 to 45% on June 30, 2009. We tend to allocate more portfolio assets to interest bearing debt when debt yields are more attractive. Approximately 20% of portfolio assets were invested in interest-bearing debt and near-cash assets on September 30, 2025. The remaining 80% of portfolio assets were invested in equities as previously described.

The outlook for financial asset returns remains uncertain. Earnings and other valuation multiples for most segments of the U.S. equity market are above historic averages. The “real” yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note (September 30 market yield on 10-year UST of 4.15% minus the August core Consumer Price Index of 3.1%) is positive at +1.05%. The average “real yield” on 10-year U.S. Treasury notes over the last 30 years was +1.25%. The absolute number of unemployed workers in the U.S. has been rising, making additional policy rate cuts probable despite inflation running above Federal Reserve targets. Recent progress to reach the Fed’s inflation targets has been difficult to achieve, complicated by uncertainty around tariffs and curtailments in the number of foreign-born workers in the U.S. labor force. A large supply of new government debt being issued plus ongoing corporate borrowing present challenges to further reductions in longer-maturity bond yields unless the U.S. economy weakens considerably in the coming quarters.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate an uncertain global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. There are clear indications that global trade flows are being significantly altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among multinational producers of tradeable goods will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions. We are optimistic about the future return potential of Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s assets. Why?

Virtually all the businesses in your portfolio retain their market positions, providing important products and services that generate cash flows to pay attractive dividends, today and in the future. We believe they are valued very attractively in relation to their own histories and relative to other assets.

The weighted harmonic average 2025 consensus expected price/earnings ratio for Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s equity portfolio, tabulated using FactSet data, was approximately 13.6x as of September 30, 2025, well below the 20.8x price/earnings ratio of the MSCI All Country World Index using 2025 expected EPS. Income Builder’s 4.3% weighted average equity portfolio dividend yield significantly exceeds the 1.8% dividend yield of the MSCI Index. We believe your Income Builder portfolio incorporates significant intrinsic value. The bond portion of your fund’s portfolio has a duration of approximately 4.1 years and a yield to maturity/call of approximately 7%.

Thank you for being a shareholder of Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund. Remember that you can review additional information about your portfolio by going to our website, www.thornburg.com.

data.title: Equinox Gold: Hitting The Sweet Spot In Mid-Tier Mining
data.description:
Among the gold companies I follow and cover, Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) has quietly become one of the more interesting mid-tier names in the space. In less than a decade, Equinox has grown from a single-asset miner into a diversified producers with a solid footprint across the Americas.

The portfolio now spans Canada, the U.S., Brazil, and Nicaragua — anchored of course by the Greenstone, Mesquite, Aurizona, and newly commissioned Valentine mines.

The June 2025 acquisition of Calibre Mining really changed the shape of Equinox in my opinion. It added the La Libertad and El Limón complexes in Nicaragua, along with the Valentine Mine in Canada — which poured first gold on the 14th of September. What this deal did was, is that it shifted Equinox's centre of gravity toward North America, lowered its cost base, and gave investors a clear line of sight to roughly one million ounces of annual production by 2026. However, a few of the older assets are still in flux — Castle Mountain remains in the permitting phase for its next expansion, while Los Filos has been moved into developments as Equinox reassess that asset's long-term potential.

Long-Term Trends

As can be expected for a young miner, production and margins have been somewhat volatile — but the last two years saw improving metrics on both accounts.

2025 is expected to be even better. Equinox has guided for 785koz-915koz of gold production in 2025 at an AISC of between $1,800/oz-$1,900/oz.

Portfolio Of Assets - Broken Down By Region

Canada

Greenstone

Greenstone is an open-pit mine in Ontario, which is expected to produce an average of 330k ounces of gold annually over its initial 15-year mine life - and will stand as Equinox's largest, and lowest-cost asset when running at full steam. The ramp-up in production has been progressing well. In Q3'25 Greenstone produced 56koz of gold at an AISC of $1,975/oz.

Equinox initially took a 60% stake in Greenstone in 2021, partnering with Orion, who held the remaining 40% stake. By May 2024, Equinox had bought out Orion to take full control, with the mine producing its first gold that same month.

Valentine

Valentine (which also formed part of the recent Calibre acquisition) is also set to become a really important asset in Equinox's portfolio. First gold was poured in September, and the mine is expected to reach nameplate capacity by the 2nd quarter of 2026.

Nicaragua

El Limon and La Libertad

El Limon and La Libertad were both acquired as part of the Calibre Acquisition — and together produced 71,119koz in Q3'25 at an AISC per ounce sold of $1,507.

Brazil

Aurizona

In April 2024, heavy rainfall at the Aurizona site triggered a "geotechnical event" that resulted in the plant standing idle for roughly eight weeks — a setback that required Equinox to revise its mine plan for this asset.

Recent production and cost trends have been uninspiring, but in the Q3 Earnings report, management remarked:

The Company is advancing permitting, exploration and studies related to an expansion that is expected to extend the mine life and increase annual gold production with development of an underground mine and satellite open pit deposits that would operate concurrently with the existing open pit mine.

Bahia Complex: Fazenda

Bahia Complex: Santa Luz

The graphs above for Fazenda and Santa Luz don't go all the way to Q3'25. That is because Equinox started reporting results from these two mines on a consolidated bases in 2025 — as the mines are situated near one another and share many synergies.

Year-to-date (at the end of Q3'25), the two mines produced a cumulative 31,503koz of gold at an AISC of $2,136 per ounce sold.

RDM

Equinox acquired the RDM asset through its acquisition of Leagold Mining in 2020.

In explaining RDM's volatile production profile, management explain on the company website that:

RDM experienced a number of operating disruptions in Q1 and Q2 2022 as the result of regulatory issues related to the expansion of the mine’s tailings storage facility. In July 2022, Equinox Gold made the decision to focus on processing low-grade stockpiles and perform owner mining to reduce costs while the tailings storage facility expansion is underway. As a result, RDM production was lower and costs higher in 2022 than in normal years. For 2023, Equinox Gold has resumed owner-operated mining but on a smaller scale than in previous years to reduce capital costs. Production at RDM in 2025 is forecast at 50,000 to 60,000 ounces of gold at all-in sustaining costs between $1,880 to $1,980 per ounce of gold sold.

USA

Mesquite Mine (California)

Mesquite was Equinox's first operating mine. Over the past decade, Mesquite has produced an average of approximately 125koz gold ounces annually. 2025 production is guided to come in between 90koz-105koz at an AISC of between $1,725-$1,825 per ounce.

Pan Mine (Nevada)

Pan Mine was acquired on the 17th of June 2025 — and contributed 11,877 gold ounces between 17 June and 30 September at an AISC per ounce sold of $1,629.

Growth Pipeline

I have already mentioned the Valentine Mine which is expected to ramp up to nameplate capacity by the 2nd quarter of 2026.

EQX has been making steady progress with its Castle Mountain expansion. During Q1, the company signed memorandums of understanding to start work on the environmental impact statement. However, the bigger development came in June, when Castle Mountain was officially accepted as a "FAST-41 project" by the U.S. Federal Permitting Council — a designation that should help streamline reviews and tighten up timelines. According to the current FAST-41 dashboard, federal permitting is expected to conclude by December 2026, which gives investors a more realistic window for when construction decisions might come into focus. It is still a long road ahead, but the project continues to move in the right direction — and with a bit more regulatory clarity now in hand.

Los Filos remains a bit more complicated, and probably the most politically sensitive asset in EQX's portfolio. Operations were suspended indefinitely in April after the land access agreement with the Carrizalillo community expired at the end of March. That was a critical setback since the CIL plant — central to the 2022 expansion plan — was meant to be built on Carrizalillo land. Without renewed access, the expansion is effectively on pause. Still, its not all dead in the water. In June, EQX reached new long-term agreements with the other two host communities, Mezcala and Xochipala, which at least keeps the door open to alternative development paths. Exploration work is set to continue in Q4, alongside engineering studies evaluating other possible sites for the carbon-in-leach plant. Any serious investment decisions will obviously hinge on those results.

Risks

Equinox's exciting growth story is not without risks. Like I mentioned above, Castle Mountain is still navigating regulatory approvals, while Los Filos faces ongoing community and permitting uncertainties that could delay future development. Operational disruptions, like the 2024 geotechnical event in Aurizona, highlight how environmental and weather-related factors can materially impact production and costs. What also concerns me a bit around EQX, is its somewhat shaky cost discipline — but I expect this to improve once the newer assets in Canada are fully up and running.

Valuation

I have selected a set of mid-tier gold producers as peers for Equinox to assess EQX's relative valuation against. These include B2Gold, Alamos Gold, IAMGOLD, Eldorado Gold, New Gold, SSR Mining, OceanaGold, Torex Gold, Regis Resources, and Northern Star Resources. I chose these peers because, like EQX, they operate primarily in the 300koz-1000koz annual production range, are actively developing growth projects, and face similar operational risks.

I like to look at FWD EV/EBITDA metrics when valuing miners as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Over the past five years, EQX traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 6% above its peers, on average.

Currently, EQX is trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio just 4% above the peer group average — suggesting to me that EQX is about fairly valued here, on a relative basis.

The Technical Take

Even with Equinox's recent strong run, the technicals still suggest a solid setup forming here. EQX is again trading above its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving average, while the MACD indicator is flashing a bullish signal again.

Analyst Recommendations

9 out of 9 reporting analysts on the Bloomberg terminal have a "Buy" rating for Equinox — with an average 12-month price target that is 25.9% above where shares are currently trading.

Conclusion

I think Equinox is now one of the more compelling mid-tier producers. With Greenstone ramping up, and Valentine now pouring, I think the company is on track for roughly $1Moz annual production by 2026. Current valuation looks fair, and the technicals look enticing. I am initiating coverage with a "Buy" rating, and agree with the consensus $15.54 price target among wall street analysts — which suggests roughly 26% upside from current levels.

data.title: Shift4: Panic Sell Or Opportunistic Buy?
data.description:
Introduction

From my initial public coverage of Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR) in May 2024, Shift4 stock rallied from the $60s to the $120s by early 2025 on the back of continued robust business performance (supported by organic & inorganic expansion):

However, Shift4's announcement of a $2.5B acquisition of specialty payments and technology provider "Global Blue" in February 2025 triggered a massive sell-off in FOUR stock, which was then exacerbated by the "Liberation Day" broad market sell-off during April. While FOUR stock rebounded strongly along with the stock market in subsequent weeks over the summer, Shift4's Q2 2025 financial results and details of its $3.3B capital raise ($1.3B Senior Notes + $1B Convertible Preferred Stock (10M shares mandatory dilution) + $1B Floating Rate Term Loan) irked investors, leaving FOUR stock reeling in the $70s once again ahead of last week's quarterly report—wherein the fintech company missed headline estimates.

Now, in the immediate aftermath of Shift4's Q3 2025 report, FOUR temporarily dropped to a new 52-week low of ~$61 per share but has since rebounded back to the mid-$70s. In today's note, we shall review Shift4's Q3 2025 earnings report and reevaluate its long-term and near-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision. Let's go!

Shift4: By The Numbers (Q3 2025)

For Q3 2025, Shift4 marginally missed headline top- and bottom-line consensus estimates amid macro-induced consumer spending jitters, with revenue coming in at $1.18B (+29.4% y/y, miss by ~$4M) and non-GAAP EPS landing at $1.47 per share (miss by $0.01).

That said, Shift4's underlying business trends remained positive, with the company delivering healthy growth across end-to-end Payment Volume ($54.7B, up 26% y/y), GRLNF ($589.2M, +61% y/y), and adj. EBITDA ($292.1M, +56% y/y).

Now, while the step up in Shift4's financial performance in Q3 was primarily a function of inorganic growth brought on by the successful close of the Global Blue acquisition on 3rd July 2025, Shift4's organic GRLNF growth was also robust at +19% y/y.

Looking forward, Shift4's management narrowed FY-2025 guidance ranges -

  • Volume: $207-210B (+26-27% y/y) [from $200-220B]
  • GRLNF: $1.98-2.02B (+46-49% y/y) [from $1.965-2.035B]
  • Adj. EBITDA: $970-985M (+43-45% y/y) [from $960-990M]

with management highlighting a weakening consumer spending environment in core verticals -

In Q3 and early Q4 we experienced modestly worsening same-store-sales from our restaurant and hotel customers, but increased contribution from new verticals and geographies. As we’ve said in the past, while not immune to consumer spending levels, our massive cross-sell funnel affords us more resiliency during periods of economic uncertainty.

- Taylor Lauber, Shift4 CEO, in Q3 2025 shareholder letter.

As you may know, Shift4 acquired Global Blue to significantly expand its global reach and enhance its integrated payment offerings, particularly in luxury retail and travel sectors. Now, in recent months, Mr. Market has doled out a brutal punishment on Shift4's stock ever since the Global Blue acquisition was announced; however, this particular transaction has diversified Shift4's business mix—categorically and geographically—with exposure to the luxury retail sector reducing business volatility tied to consumer spending.

With Global Blue, Shift4 now has the opportunity to cross-sell to thousands of luxury retail customers. Now, while the bigger luxury brands like Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMUY) are well-served by the likes of Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) and Stripe (i.e., taking market share will be an uphill battle), Shift4 could certainly win several boutique firms on Global Blue's customer roster. As such, I am excited to follow this piece of the business in 2026.

As of Q3 2025, Shift4's contracted volume backlog stood at $35B, and its cross-sell payments funnel has expanded to >$1T. Over the medium term (3-4 years), Shift4 is targeting 30% CAGR growth in GRLNF and adj. EBITDA, with annual free cash flow projected to exceed $1B+ by 2028-29.

From a business standpoint, Shift4 has market-leading products in multiple categories. With international expansion still in early stages, Shift4's profitable growth story has many chapters left.

The Balance Sheet Concerns Are Overblown

Over recent quarters, Shift4 has advanced its international expansion plans via an aggressive M&A strategy that has also seen it acquire the likes of Vectron (Germany, Europe), Eigen (Canada), and SmartPay (New Zealand, Australia) in addition to the Global Blue acquisition.

As a result, Shift4's financial debt has ballooned to $4.8 billion as of the end of Q3 2025, which is roughly 5x 2025E adjusted EBITDA of $978M. Now, while this elevated leverage position is less than ideal due to higher interest costs, it is certainly not untenable.

For starters, Shift4 is a highly cash flow-generative business (FY-25E FCF: $488M, FY-28E FCF: $1B+), and the plan for Global Blue does not involve legacy business deprecation like past acquisitions, i.e., Global Blue's cash generation will support Shift4's future debt repayment efforts.

Now, with each M&A transaction, we have integration risks. And these risks are especially elevated in the Shift4-Global Blue combo due to cultural differences. However, Shift4's 27+ year history is laden with several M&A transactions, and I trust Jared Isaacman (now Executive Chairman), Taylor Lauber (CEO), and the Shift4 executive leadership team to deliver success once again!

Importantly, they have ample time to get things right. Having retired its 2026 Convertible Senior Notes [$450M] in Q2 2025 using some of the proceeds of its $3.3B capital raise, Shift4's debt repayment maturities are now staggered farther into the future.

Yes, Shift4 has a $690M debt repayment due in December 2025; the company should be able to cover it with cash (avoiding dilution from the converts). And, looking beyond that, Shift4 is expected to generate >$1B in annual FCF by 2028-29, which will be more than adequate to meet its longer-dated debt obligations.

Now, Shift4's recently-closed acquisition of SmartPay (closed on 4th November 2025, deal size ~$180M) and impending acquisition of Bambora (expected close Q1 2026, deal size unknown) aren't factored into the math here today; however, as I see it, Shift4's current liquidity and robust free cash flow generation provide adequate support for management's aggressive M&A push.

And Shift4's management and BOD seem to agree. After all, Shift4 just announced a new $1B share repurchase plan, focused on managing dilution.

At ~$73.18 per share (or $7.29B), Shift4 is trading at ~15x 2025E FCF, which is dirt cheap for a company growing as fast as Shift4. As such, I think the new stock buyback program makes total sense and should instill a high floor for FOUR stock.

Shift4 Fair Value And Expected Return

According to TQI's Valuation Model, FOUR stock is worth ~$136 per share, which is roughly 86% above its current level.

Additional Notes:

1) For the model, I have considered a $4.23B revenue base (2025E revenue) and assumed a reasonable 15% CAGR sales growth over the 2026-30 5-year modeling period.

2) While Shift4's FY-25E adj. FCF of $488M represents an adj. FCF margin of ~11.5%, I am sticking with our optimized FCF margin assumption of 15% due to an expectation of operational leverage and improved cash flow conversion for Shift4 at a larger business scale in the future.

3) Furthermore, to account for idiosyncratic risks tied to the integration of multiple M&A transactions (and consequent leverage position), I have increased the model's required IRR from 15% to 20%—thereby instilling a margin of safety in the model.

4) The share count has been raised to 99.6M shares, factoring in Shift4's $1B mandatory Convertible Series A Preferred Stock (FOUR.PR.A).

Based on a reasonable exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, I see Shift4 stock rising to $277.51 per share over the next five years at a CAGR rate of 30.55%.

With its 5-year expected CAGR return being significantly higher than TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%, I continue to view Shift4 as an asymmetric investment for long-term investors at current levels.

FOUR Technical Setup

From a technical standpoint, Shift4 stock appears to be forming a massive triangle pattern. Now, triangles can break in either direction; however, with FOUR stocks rebounding off of the multi-year uptrend line (marked in green arrow) in recent days and weekly RSI levels indicating "oversold" conditions, I hold a constructive technical view on Shift4 stock.

Also, as per Elliot Wave Theory, Shift4 appears to have completed a 5-wave move to the downside when it tested the long-term support trendline in the low-$60s. Consequently, we can expect a move back up to the "wave-4" high around ~$90 per share in FOUR in the near-term.

And this constructive technical view is supported by Shift4's healthy business momentum and significant stock undervaluation.

Now, given heightened macroeconomic uncertainties, I remain open-minded here, but I really do like the odds of a bullish move.

Final Thoughts

Shift4 is primed for several years of rapid, profitable growth. The stock is deeply undervalued, and while its technical setup is directionally unclear, FOUR stock is sitting right above its multi-year bullish trendline. On the back of a ~40% dip off its early 2025 highs, I view Shift4's long-term risk/reward as truly asymmetric once again.

Key Takeaway: I continue to rate Shift4 a "Strong Buy" in the $70s.

As always, thank you for reading, and happy investing. Please feel free to share any questions, concerns, or thoughts in the comments section below.

data.title: Hemisphere Energy: Strong Balance Sheet, Weak Oil Environment
data.description:
Hemisphere Energy (OTCQX:HMENF) remains one of the few interesting microcap Canadian oil stocks. I like it as it maintains its positive cash flow even, when oil prices are down. Yet, since it's Q1 2025, it showed that the financial model is still functioning, but its resilience is decreasing.

The first signs that the market was changing were clear in spring - revenue growth moderated, realized prices improved to C$79/boe from C$74 a year ago, but the company was hit by expensive polymers, which are needed for enhanced oil recovery technology. This leads me to doubt dividend sustainability, and I estimate that every additional $5 change in WTI price can directly determine whether the company maintains excess FCF or just breaks even. As of Q2 netbacks were weaker at C$42.77/boe, and the realized price was C$70.06/boe. I believe that Q3 will show even more pressure, and it can affect stock price negatively.

Liquidity seems healthy. The company does not have debt and has around C$14 million, and a C$35 million credit line remains unused. This gives flexibility, which lets them not only sustain the dividend program, but also survive fluctuating oil prices. That is why, I like it; it has conservative balance, and it does make it different from other Canadian micro-E&P competitors, which most of the time rely on credit. At the same time, this safety has a price, as Hemisphere could avoid investments and aggressive drilling network expansion, when free cash flow is not secured with better prices.

Structurally, financial situation is dependent on one basin—Atlee Buffalo, which makes up around 97% of total production. While, yes, it gives operational effectiveness and cost control, it limits diversification. One serious technological or supply chain disruption (for example, polymer injection chain) could briefly knock out an entire company's cash flow. In 2024-2025, polymer flood activity increased operating costs, which is decreasing netbacks. This factor, combined with weaker realized prices (~70 CAD/bbl vs. 80 CAD/bbl in 2024), might put pressure on FCF yield.

The dividend program remains the main management priority. Yearly base payout (C$0.10 per share), I think, is still sustainable even at WTI being $60, but to be fair, I doubt that special dividends will continue. Management is signalling, that any additional capital distribution depends on WTI staying above $70. While the share buyback program is still ongoing, I feel it might slow down (although they have bought around 1.3 million shares).

If we look at a wider view, Hemisphere's financial situation reflects typical low leverage but cyclical sensitivity. The company has enough capital to survive at least 12-18 months with lower commodity prices, but I don't see them growing until the WTI price jumps back up. The biggest danger is not losing its liquidity, but long-term cash flow shrinking, which would force them to decrease dividends.

All in all, financially the company is still healthy, but sensitive. It has all the signs of a stable company, but its profitability buffer evaporates together with oil prices. Until WTI reaches $70 again, I believe they are going to stand in defensive positions - more of a stability story, not a growth story.

Assets

Atlee Buffalo

Hemisphere operations depend on one concentrated asset - Atlee Buffalo basin in southeast Alberta. Like I mentioned, this is not only the company’s core, but its financial foundation. Around 97% of production is made here, and Hemisphere has 100% operational and property rights, so it is controlling capital distribution and technology decisions.

Atlee Buffalo is made out of two pools, Upper Mannville F and G pools. Polymer EOR project success made Atlee Buffalo a cash-flow factory. The company basically works in a harvest regime: investments in drilling are going down, and a bigger part of FCF is directed to dividends and share buybacks. Nevertheless, project needs maintenance; polymers need to be supplied regularly. As I said before, polymer flood activity increased operating costs in 2025.

Atlee Buffalo has almost 10 million barrels of proved (1P) reserves, and 2P reserves are over 14.5 million barrels. Proved Developed Producing reaches more than 9 million boe with quite low additional capital (C$16 million). This shows that the project is mature, has low sustaining capital needs, and is a stable, free cash flow generating asset. Why am I saying that this is a long-life-span asset? As with the current tempo of 3800 boe/d, the reserves should last 8-10 years. From what I have seen in the market, similar companies have around 5-7 years.

Another important part is that due to EOR technology, big part of 2P reserves has the potential to become PDP category reserves without significant investments. Due to this conversion ratio, I believe Hemisphere has a competitive advantage. When a company can maintain reservoir pressure and increase recovery rate, the reserves not only increase in lifespan but also become even more cash flow generating.

Marsden Project

This one is a pilot expansion zone. Five wells were drilled (two injectors and three production wells), and a new injection facility was also built. This is an experimental project that lets Hemisphere test polymer flooding effectiveness in a new geological environment. Even though Marsden makes up a few percent of the company's reserves, a successful project could give Hemisphere a second leg - a second production center and additional reserve base. They expect a reaction to the injection by the end of 2025, and I am looking forward to it, as it might become a new catalyst.

Hemisphere has very few assets, as you can see, but at least I expect that it is profitable when WTI is above $65-70. One basin means less diversification. On the other hand, polymeric technology is a double-edged sword; it gives lifespan, but requires stability of raw material supply and also capital discipline. The company is very concentrated, and this is a major red flag I have when oil prices are fluctuating.

Capital Effectiveness And Dividends

One of the reasons why the company can generate quite stable cash flow even in fluctuating oil is due to F&D price, which is C$1.9-2.1/boe, when in the market I know a lot of companies that have it around C$6-8/boe. This shows that Atlee Buffalo Basin has a high return coefficient - low well density, good recovery rate, and low infrastructure costs let them have a strong return on invested capital.

The company’s sustaining CapEx is just C$4-6 million a year, which is around 20-25% AFF. Structure like this is exceptionally effective, as Hemisphere can finance dividends. Capital is distributed only to maintaining production and polymer injection effectiveness, not expansion.

Dividends

This is the part where I see a lot of risk. Currently they are sustainable, but their future belongs to WTI dynamics. If it stays at $60/bbl, base C$0.10 annual payout should still be covered, but I estimate that the coverage ratio decreased to 1.5-2x. If WTI prices fell to $55 or lower (which might happen, given that while I write this article, WTI is -4% on the daily chart), financing dividends from free cash flow would become difficult. Also, I believe special dividends might be paused.

In short, current dividends stand on effectiveness, not growth. This means that Hemisphere is a defensive income play, which will continue paying out, as long as FCF remains positive, but any long-term WTI price fall might erase the financial buffer. Also, check out the Seeking Alpha warning.

Valuation

Hemisphere Energy today is valued moderately. EV/EBITDA (TTM) is around 3.4x, when Gear Energy (KGEI) is valued at 4.09x, and Headwater Exploration (OTCQX:ALVOF) - 4.11x. This shows, that market values Hemisphere with around a 20-25% discount, even though the balance is strong. PE (TTM) at around 6.5x, compared to 7.8x for KGEI and 8.4x for ALVOF, EV/Sales (FWD) - 1.95x, lower than most of the competitors.

Price to Cash Flow (TTM) reaches 3.45x, when Gear Energy is 4.73x or Evolution Petroleum (EPM) at 4.73. This proves that Hemisphere is valued as a cash-efficient and low growth company, but overall valuation is quite moderate, and I would even say cheap given its sustainable dividend position.

Summary

Hemisphere Energy remains financially healthy, but very cyclical and sensitive. The strengths are its strong balance sheet, low CapEx need, and sustainable dividends, which might still be covered even at WTI $60. Main asset Atlee Buffalo generates stable cash flows, but dependency on one asset and polymer pricing are the main risk.

My position remains hold, it is attractive for investors that are searching for a dividend defensive position with low financial risk, but it's not suitable for those looking for growth. Also, even for dividend investing, it's risky and should be looked at as a speculation.

data.title: Dell: From AI Hype To Real Margins - $180 In Sight
data.description:
The stock's upside is in margins, not servers

My concern with Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) isn't how many AI services it can sell, but how much it can earn (margins). Ultimately, as we all know, it's profits that drive stocks up or down, not volumes (of servers, in this case). A turning point could come if the server and infrastructure division (called the "ISG" division) manages to achieve stable and consistent operating margins between 10 and 14 percent in the coming quarters. For me, it remains the true long-term growth driver.

What will you find in this article?

In this article, I'll analyze:

  • How much AI-related orders, such as those from Microsoft or IREN, are truly creating value for Dell, and how much they are eroding margins.
  • I'll analyze the "attach" component (i.e., how much storage and services it manages to sell alongside AI servers), the effect of value engineering on Blackwell systems, and what all this means for prices and profitability. My goal is to analyze whether AI is driving profits or just revenue.
  • Then we'll move on to valuation, because Dell is still priced at low multiples today.
  • I'll highlight the aspects I'm interested in seeing in the quarterly results at the end of November. My focus will be on: a) gross margin trends, b) ISG division margins, c) the mix of enterprise and sovereign customers, and d) the outlook for next year.
  • Risks and opportunities will close this analysis. We'll discuss data center energy availability, white-box pricing pressure, and PC-side execution.
  • For those who follow the stock, this could be an interesting in-depth analysis to read. As always, please give me your feedback in the comments and tell me what you think of the company, its stock positioning, and its prospects.

Latest quarterly report

I​‍​‌‍​‍‌ won't get into it too much here; the last quarterly report was published in August, and we are a lot closer to the next one that will be released on the 25th of this month. I'll just give a brief rundown of the things that were very successful and those that were ​‍​‌‍​‍‌not.

The numbers published in the quarterly report are truly impressive. There's no point in reporting each figure. I'll tell you what I think. Right now, artificial intelligence has finally become one of the main drivers of company revenue. Orders and shipments were impressive in terms of overall revenue. For more information, I recommend carefully reading the report.

In the call with analysts, management emphasized that the pipeline is far greater than the current order book, so we're not talking about imaginary orders but actual demand that has already been satisfied. Systems like the GB200, NVL72, and GB300 are in operation.

The ISG division I mentioned in the introduction to this article (the one that hosts AI servers and infrastructure) has grown significantly, up 44%, which is undoubtedly significant. As an analyst, I'm not going to be impressed or enamored by these numbers; we need to see continuity, and, above all, what matters now is no longer the speed of volumes but the quality of margins. As long as Dell continues to sell AI servers with compressed profit profiles, growth will remain more "industrial" than "financial". Great for revenue, less so for value generation.

If the company manages to stabilize its ISG margin above 10% in the coming quarters and grow its storage and services component alongside its servers, then yes, I'd feel more at ease.

Margins remain a work in progress. Gross margin remained more or less unchanged, although as a percentage of revenue, it fell to 21.4% (from 22% in the previous quarter). One-time costs for expediting and reconfiguration likely weighed negatively on Blackwell.

What I want to see in the next quarterly report

In the next quarterly report, I need to see a couple of things materialize. Above all, I want to see margins recover. So far, Dell hasn't given in to the temptation to cut prices just to boost volume. On the contrary, it has defended its price list, and this has begun to be reflected in the financial statements. Now we need to see how the situation evolves, whether for better or worse.

The other important point is that the storage and services "attachment" (i.e., the accessory sold with servers) is becoming automatic, no longer tied to individual projects or customized offerings. This means more recurring revenues and more stable margins. It's the part that, if properly integrated, can create continuity and long-term visibility.

Finally, it's becoming clear that the healthiest demand comes from the enterprise and sovereign world, not just from the large hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, etc.). This is an important aspect, because the enterprise and sovereign world guarantees Dell a certain continuity and pricing power, while the latter brings huge volumes but often slim margins.

I'm interested to know if Blackwell's value engineering is actually affecting costs and if the supply chain has changed from "chasing demand at all costs" to a "structured and sustainable" strategy.

Valuation

I'll start with EPS, as always. Expectations for the coming years are as follows.

EPS for 2026 is $9.55, and I don't make any specific additional comments on this number. The key is to understand where Dell can naturally reach without any particular scenarios or sudden changes. For FY27, EPS is $11.3 (source: Bloomberg Consensus), and I don't have much to say here either, because this figure can be reached simply by removing one-off items and improving the mix toward enterprise and sovereign customers (as I described earlier) and with some value engineering at Blackwell, which lowers the cost per rack. So far, it's just normalization. Time will be your best ally.

In my opinion, the market continues to underestimate two important levers:

  • structural attachment
  • and buybacks.

"Structural​‍​‌‍​‍‌ attach" refers to the practice of consistently and automatically coupling storage, networking, and services with hardware that is sold, rather than just in special projects. So, Business continuity. Long-term visibility. No surprises. ​‍​That's what it means for me. If this integration becomes structural, it will change the quality of the business, and every additional percentage point of attachment translates into higher margins, more predictable revenues, and less dependence on hardware cycles.

Then comes the question of capital. Dell vowed in its earnings presentation to share over 80% of its free cash flow with its stockholders, as you can see here.

At these prices, if the pace of buybacks remains similar, a 3–4% annual increase in basic EPS due solely to the lower number of shares outstanding is entirely realistic.

Combining the two levers—i.e., higher revenues from services and storage and a regular buyback—I arrive at a 2027 EPS of around $12 per share.

From there, the choice is all about the multiple. I've never fallen in love with individual stocks, and this won't be the case either. In my opinion, with a P/E of 15x, the stock reflects healthy growth and significant cash generation. This isn't fantasy, but realistic, just above the tech sector median (source: Seeking Alpha). This value recognizes the company's potential in terms of artificial intelligence when it translates into real profits, not just volumes.

So we said $12 EPS and a 15x multiple. The fair price, according to my valuation, is $180 per share.

I'm changing my approach for the ultimate test. I need to be sure of what I'm doing.

  • If I stay closer to the analyst consensus ($11.3), at 15x I get $170 per share.
  • If the overall market slows down, then I pay 14 times, and with $12 I get $168 per share;
  • With $11.3, we get $158.

To be completely honest, the true operational bear case for me is 13x on $11.3 (a scenario that occurs if the mix doesn't improve and the attach is weak). In that case, the price is $146.

Risks

So far, I've highlighted the positive aspects and therefore the main opportunities. As always, no investment is risk-free, and in my opinion, the main one is margins, not revenues. My main concern, in fact, is the new Blackwell/NVL72 racks, which remain expensive to assemble, meaning the company could experience flat margin growth, favoring higher revenues and volumes. To overcome this limitation, the company must implement a solid cost optimization strategy (value engineering, to be clear); otherwise, it risks continuing to grow revenues but with stagnant margins. Shareholders aren't interested in sales but in how many dollars are earned from each sale.

Another risk concerns AI-related demand, which can be uneven. Dell has a massive backlog, but deliveries depend on practical factors such as power availability, cooling systems, and customer installation times. If any of these factors slow down, even for just a few weeks, it could be enough to result in a weaker-than-expected quarter.

Margins, cash, and a $180 reality check

The opportunities are there, especially at these prices, especially after a stock price reversal.

Dell has to concentrate on turning artificial intelligence into a real driver not only of revenue but also—and most especially—of margin. Clearly the first element that could be helpful in this case is "attach", as I said before.

Then, the situation in the capital is very favorable. The last earnings say Dell is a company that will probably repay its shareholders over 80% of the adjusted free cash flow through dividends and buybacks. Therefore, any operational improvement in ISG will lead to a very rapid increase in EPS, thus bringing the revaluation point closer to my $180.

I'll add a detail that the market is ignoring or not considering adequately, in my opinion. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌ deal with IREN and Microsoft (that comprises GB300 components, networking, and services) is not just a source of increased revenue, but it also implies an additional $0.30-$0.40 of EPS that is quite significant on a yearly ​‍​‌‍​‍‌basis.

At these levels, I see a favorable risk/reward ratio for anyone entering at this level. Personally, I've been in the market longer (although this is the first time I've written about Dell on this platform).

data.title: 2 Years Of The Rise Of The 'Magnificent Seven' Tech Stocks
data.description:
The seven companies that make up the S&P 500's "Magnificent Seven" stocks have been on the rise for some time.

Two years ago, they combined to account for 30% of the total valuation of the S&P 500 (SPX). And to be sure, several of the members of this elite group of high-flying companies were already among the biggest components of the index before they became collectively known as the "Magnificent 7". The weren't known by that label until 2023, when Bank of American analyst Michael Hartnett first deployed the phrase with respect to "a basket of seven stocks: Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA)".

Since then, these mega-cap stocks have earned the moniker by growing even larger. Through October 2025, they have collectively grown to account for 37% of the entire market capitalization of the S&P 500.

Pretty amazing stuff when you realize that Tesla wasn't even a publicly-traded company until 2010 and Nvidia's explosive growth because of its Artificial Intelligence (AI) enabling computer chip technology began in 2023.

The following pair of charts illustrates the growth of the Magnificent Seven stocks over the past two years. The first chart shows how their value has increased and driven the growth of the S&P 500 index itself. The second chart shows how their collective share of the S&P 500 index has likewise grown.

They're able to drive the total valuation of the S&P 500 because their market capitalizations make up such a large share of the index. In today's market, what happens to the S&P 500 index is almost all about what's happening with the Magnificent Seven stocks.

How long might that last? Time will tell, but it helps put today's record concentration of market capitalization by seven companies into some context, the last time the S&P 500 index (or rather, its predecessor index, the S&P 90) had such a large percentage of its total value represented by such a small number of stocks was back in 1932.

References

Standard & Poor's. S&P Market Attributes Web File. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 3 November 2025.

Original Post
